description: Curated Intelligence Emotions terms that get a one-sentence gloss on first use per session. Terms not on this list are assumed plain-English enough. Glosses below are the canonical phrasings; adapt naturally, keep the meaning.

terms.gloss: your mental-fitness quotient — roughly, the percentage of time your mind is helping you rather than sabotaging you
a 10-second shift of attention to a physical sensation — breath, fingertips, sound — that quiets the saboteur brain and strengthens the Sage
an automatic negative mental pattern — everyone has a set of ten, led by the Judge
the inner voice that finds fault with everything — yourself, others, your circumstances
the anxious need to take charge and control how everything turns out
the pattern that makes your self-worth depend on your latest performance
the pattern that treats feelings — yours and everyone's — as noise to be analyzed away
the pattern that scans for danger nonstop and never lets you rest
the pattern that earns acceptance by helping and flattering while your own needs go unsaid
the pattern that's always chasing the next exciting thing instead of being here
perfectionism — the need for order and exactness taken past the point of usefulness
the pattern that dwells in painful feelings as a way of being seen
the pattern that stays with the pleasant and dodges the hard task or conversation
the part of your mind that meets any circumstance with curiosity, compassion, and clear-eyed action
the stance that any circumstance can be turned into a gift or opportunity — not that it's fine, but that something useful can be made of it
the Sage power of compassion for yourself and others
the Sage power of open curiosity — looking at what's actually there without an agenda
the Sage power of generating genuinely new options
the Sage power of choosing the path that fits your deeper values
the Sage power of moving into action without the saboteurs steering
a Sage technique: find three ways a setback could become a gift — what it teaches, what it strengthens, what it sets in motion
an Empathize technique: picture the person — or yourself — as the child they once were
an Explore technique: study the situation like a field researcher who only wants to see what's really there
a Navigate technique: look back at this choice from the end of your life and see which option you'd respect
an Activate technique: name in advance exactly how your saboteurs will try to derail the plan
catching a saboteur in the act and naming it — 'ah, that's my Judge' — which loosens its grip
the false claim a saboteur makes that feels like truth in the moment
the situation, person, or thought that wakes a saboteur up
a vague intention turned into a concrete 21-day practice plan with daily reps and check-ins
how often you catch a saboteur in the act versus only noticing in hindsight — both count, catching live is the trained skill
the capacity to meet life's challenges with a positive rather than negative mindset — built with reps, like physical fitness
the level of mental fitness — around 75% PQ in the model's framing — where your mind starts lifting you more than it weighs you down
